coredata.dc:title: Inherent photocatalytic activity of luminescent multi-doped carbon dots manufactured from expired medicine and its application for efficient water remediation and nanosensing
coredata.dc:creator: Kamel, Gehad N.
El-Shaheny, Rania
Abo Shabana, Rasha
Hassan, Ahmed H.E.
coredata.dc:description: A fast and green synthesis of multi-doped carbon dots (multi-doped CDs) has been accomplished within 6 min of microwave treatment of a mixture of expired medicine, Vitamin B1, and phosphate salt as a feedstock. Characterization of the prepared quantum dots revealed multi-doping with nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, sodium, and chlorine. The prepared multi-doped CDs were applied for dual functions as an efficient photocatalyst and a sensitive fluorescence nanosensor that are not restricted by toxicity, cost, or stability. The efficient light absorption, electron transfer, and separation of photogenerated charge carriers within the multi-doped CDs enabled their use as an effective photocatalyst for remediation of organic dye-polluted water. Efficacious degradation of eriochrome black T dye (≈88 %) was accomplished within 80 min under visible light irradiation. Radical scavenging experiments demonstrated that the active species are hole, superoxide, and hydroxyl radicals. Furthermore, the multi-doped CDs were applied as a nanosensor for sensitive determination of the muscle relaxant drug thiocolchicoside (TCC) via fluorescence switch-off mechanism mediated by inner filter effect and dynamic quenching. The nanosensor was applied for the determination of TCC in its tablets with mean recovery % of 99.95 ± 1.55 and excellent selectivity. The present approach is a further step for circular economy applications hence it converts valueless chemical waste into valued products with useful applications.

head.ce:title: Inherent photocatalytic activity of luminescent multi-doped carbon dots manufactured from expired medicine and its application for efficient water remediation and nanosensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Medicinal Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Medicinal Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fast and green synthesis of multi-doped carbon dots (multi-doped CDs) has been accomplished within 6 min of microwave treatment of a mixture of expired medicine, Vitamin B1, and phosphate salt as a feedstock. Characterization of the prepared quantum dots revealed multi-doping with nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, sodium, and chlorine. The prepared multi-doped CDs were applied for dual functions as an efficient photocatalyst and a sensitive fluorescence nanosensor that are not restricted by toxicity, cost, or stability. The efficient light absorption, electron transfer, and separation of photogenerated charge carriers within the multi-doped CDs enabled their use as an effective photocatalyst for remediation of organic dye-polluted water. Efficacious degradation of eriochrome black T dye (≈88 %) was accomplished within 80 min under visible light irradiation. Radical scavenging experiments demonstrated that the active species are hole, superoxide, and hydroxyl radicals. Furthermore, the multi-doped CDs were applied as a nanosensor for sensitive determination of the muscle relaxant drug thiocolchicoside (TCC) via fluorescence switch-off mechanism mediated by inner filter effect and dynamic quenching. The nanosensor was applied for the determination of TCC in its tablets with mean recovery % of 99.95 ± 1.55 and excellent selectivity. The present approach is a further step for circular economy applications hence it converts valueless chemical waste into valued products with useful applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Currently, nanomaterials have become well established with the advancement of nanotechnology [1]. Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel class of nanomaterials showing prominent advantageous attributes, including exceptional water dispersibility, unique optical properties, minimal toxicity, biocompatibility, and stable photoluminescence [2]. Due to these distinctive characteristics, CDs compete with semiconductor quantum dots, fluorescence dyes, fluorophores and others nanostructure metallic forms in terms of easy functionalization, high photobleaching resistance, and high-water solubility. As a result, CDs are promising candidates for a variety of applications such as bioimaging, diagnosis, photocatalysis, biosensors, and pharmaceutical and environmental analysis [3,4]. CDs are often synthesized using a variety of precursors with high carbon contents [5]. Yet, the synthesis techniques utilized to generate CDs fall into three categories: flame combustion/oxidation of raw carbon materials, hydrothermal and carbonization of small organic molecules as carbon precursors with different reagents, achieving simultaneous fabrication and ease of surface functionalization [2]. In the realm of nanomaterial research, microwave-induced CDs manufacturing is now a developing area since this approach has many benefits including uniform energy distribution within the reaction vessel, shorter synthesis time, high reproducibility, and superb control of experimental parameters. Furthermore, it is an energetically and environmentally friendly method [6]. Because of these features, we employ this method as a green approach for the synthesis of reliable and environmentally friendly CDs.

Every year, up to one million tons of pharmaceuticals is utilized to treat both humans and animals [5]. While efforts are made to secure these medications availability, it is possible that some medications reach expiration date. The lack of efficient supply chain management, inadequate storage, and overabundance of medications are the main causes of the worrisome global increase of expired medications and pharmaceutical waste [7]. The inappropriate disposal of drug waste causes devastating effects on both the ecosystem and human health due to significant biochemical activity of these pharmaceuticals even at extremely low concentration. However, drug waste and expired drugs might serve as a cost-effective source to manufacture innovative nanostructures to find a solution, get benefits, and implementing a “Valueless-to-Wealth” strategy [5].

Thus, in this study we developed a straightforward single-step microwave-aided method to convert the outdated pharmaceutical waste of thiamine hydrochloride (vitamin B1, VB1), to value-added CDs. VB1 is used to treat Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome, beriberi, and pathophysiologic diseases linked to diabetes and hypermetabolic disorder [8]. Herein, we used expired VB1 as a carbon source for CDs synthesis. Besides, it is a source of doping heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, and chloride. Furthermore, expired sodium phosphate has also been used as a source of phosphorus. This combination furnished multi-doped CDs with unique properties such as superb water solubility, excellent stability, high fluorescence, non-toxicity, and photocatalytic ability. These features reinforce the dual functions of multi-doped CDs as a fluorescence sensor for pharmaceutical analysis and a photocatalyst for water remediation.

First, we effectively applied the generated multi-doped CDs as a nano fluorescence sensor for the quantitative determination of the muscle relaxant and anti-inflammatory drug thiocolchicoside (TCC) in pharmaceutical preparations. TCC is a colchicine analogue [9,10], chemically known as N-[1,2-dimethoxy-10-methylsulphanyl-9-oxo-3-(3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-hydroxymethyltetrahydropyran-2-yloxy)-5,6,7,9-tetrahydrobenzo[a]heptalen-7-yl]-acetamide). It is a GABA agonist powerful muscle relaxant used to treat rheumatic diseases, painful muscular spasms, traumatic conditions, and acute low back pain [11,12]. The analytical techniques published for determining TCC include high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [13,14], square wave voltametric method [15], TLC/densitometry [16,17], RP-HPLC [18], and spectrophotometric methods [19,20]. Considering the disadvantages of these approaches, such as long sample preparation time and analysis, poor sensitivity, and required specialized equipment, hence the need for a more efficient analytical technique remains a defy. In this context, fluorescence analysis has received a substantial interest due to its convenience, high sensitivity, simplicity of operation, dependable approach, and low detection limit [21]. Thus, we utilized the prepared multi-doped CDs as a fluorescence probe for rapid determination of the non-fluorescent compound TCC via a direct mix-and-read method. Since TCC absorption spectrum showed a strong overlap with the emission and excitation spectra of the multi-doped CDs, it quantitatively quenches the multi-doped CDs fluorescence via inner filter effect [22] synergized by dynamic quenching that takes place due to collision of TCC and CDs molecules [23].

Secondly, we applied the prepared multi-doped CDs as a photocatalyst for remediation of industrial wastewater. The accumulation of environmental pollutants grew with the globalization of human activity showing several detrimental negative consequences on people, animals, and the ecosystem [24]. Among the widely occurring environmental pollutants are the toxic organic dyes used in the textile industry that generate plenty of liquid waste which pollutes rivers and sewage systems [25,26]. Most of these dyes are azo compounds [27,28] such as eriochrome black T (EBT; sodium 3-hydroxy-4-[(1-hydroxynaphthalen-2-yl)diazenyl]-7-nitronaphthalene-1-sulfonate) [29]. It is an extremely dangerous and toxic dye since it produces naphthoquinone, an intermediate product, which is a potential carcinogen [30]. It is challenging to eliminate or decompose EBT because of its stable and non-degradable chemical structure [27]. A summary of its chemical and physical properties is presented in Table S1 (Supplementary Material) [29]. In this connection, some techniques are currently used including coagulation, adsorption, membrane separation, and biological approach to remove contaminants from wastewater [31]. However, these techniques have some drawbacks, as they lower the concentration of the dye in water without degrading it resulting in secondary contamination. Besides, the biological approach is time-consuming and certain dyes cannot be treated aerobically [32]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a more effective and affordable technique to remove dye pollution from wastewater. Photocatalytic degradation has an essential role in the treatment of wastewater due to its strong redox capacity, efficiency, and environmental sustainability [25]. Furthermore, it is an economical method in contrast to adsorption-based water purification strategies [33] since it can completely degrade resistant organic pollutants using only light and catalyst. Most photocatalysts utilized for water treatment depend on hybrid composites of carbon dots and semiconductors [31]. Yet, fewer reports managed to utilize CDs as a photocatalyst. Importantly, the prepared multi-doped CDs were ensured to have excellent photocatalytic activity showing superior degradation of EBT in visible light.

This research opens the door for a new strategy to eradicate two major environmental risks; pharmaceutical waste and organic dye via a value-addition approach that valorize the wastes to create new valued CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental
Apparatus
ce:section.ce:para: The multi-doped CDs were synthesized in a Sharp microwave 900-watt, model R-750MR(S) (made in Egypt). Fluorescence intensities were measured using an Agilent Technologies (Santa Clara, CA 95051, USA) Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. The absorption spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu UV–VIS Spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan). Temperature controlled water bath (Cambridge Ltd, United King) and for sonication Eumax ultrasonic bath, model SS 101H 230 (United States) were utilized. The water still used was DESA 0081 Vidrio Industrial POBEL (made in Spain). A consort NV P-901 pH meter (Belgium) was used for pH measurements.

The size and shape of the prepared multi-doped CDs have been scanned using JEOL GEM-1010 transmission electron microscope (TEM) at 80 kV. Zeta Potential Analyzer (Malvern Zetasize, Nano-zs90) was used for measuring Zeta potential of the synthesized multi-doped CDs. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analysis for the SEM (Oxford X-Max 20) which provides an idea about the elements already present. Thermo Fisher Scientific (MA, United states) Nicolet-iS10 FTIR spectrometer with attenuated total reflectance was used to perform Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FTIR) analysis. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was acquired on K-ALPHA (Themo Fisher Scientific, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemicals and solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Thiamine HCl (VB1) with expiration date of 02/2011, was collected from our laboratory (Batch No UQ80208103, European Egyptian Pharmaceuticals). Sodium orthophosphate dodecahydrate with expiration date of 05/2018, product of EL Nasr pharmaceutical chemicals Co, was used. TCC was provided by Memphis Pharmaceutical Co. (Cairo, Egypt). Boric acid was purchased from El-Gomhouria Pharmaceuticals Co. (Cairo, Egypt). Quinine sulfate was provided by Sigma-Aldrich Co. (United States). Cadmium sulfate (assay 99.9 %) and calcium chloride (assay 97 %) were obtained from PIOCHEM Co. (6th of October city, Giza, Egypt). Lab chemicals encompass sodium bicarbonate (assay 99 %), sodium carbonate (assay 97 %), sodium hydroxide, starch, KCl, NaCl, glucose, sucrose, sodium citrate, and citric acid were procured from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (United states). Paracetamol was gifted by EIPICO Co. (10th of Ramadan City, Egypt). Spiramycin was donated by Delta Pharma S.A.E (10th of Ramadan City, Egypt). Aceclofenac was purchased from European Egyptian Pharmaceuticals Co. EBT dye was purchased from PIOCHEM Co. (6th of October city, Egypt).

TCC working solution (100 μg/mL) and EBT (45 × 10−6 M) were prepared in water. Tablets containing TCC (10 mg TCC /tablet) were prepared. Britton Robinson buffer (BRB) was prepared from 0.04 M boric acid, 0.04 M orthophosphoric acid, and 0.04 M acetic acid where the pH is adjusted using 0.2 M sodium hydroxide. Multi-doped CDs working solution was prepared by dilution of 1 mL of the prepared CDs stock solution to 100 mL with distilled water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of eco-friendly multi-doped CDs from expired VB1
ce:section.ce:para: The multi-doped CDs have been synthesized via an economical, efficient, and eco-friendly method. Expired VB1 and sodium phosphate were mixed in a ratio of 1:1, w/w (0.1375 g:0.1375 g) and dissolved in 3 mL distilled water in a small beaker. The mixture was sonicated for 5 min to ensure complete dissolution. The solution was to put it in a household microwave at 900 W for 6 min. The resulting canary yellow residue was then dissolved in 20 mL distilled water after cooling to room temperature. The mixture was ultrasonicated for 20 min to agitate and solubilize the synthesized multi-doped CDs. The obtained clear solution was kept in an amber brown glass bottle and stored at 4 °C for further applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield (Φf) measurement
ce:section.ce:para:
By using quinine sulfate (QS) as a standard with 54 % quantum yield, Φ (prepared in 0.1 M aqueous solution of sulfuric acid), the Φ of the synthesized multi-doped CDs was calculated. To minimize the inner filter effect (IFE), standard solutions of quinine sulfate and the multi-doped CDs with absorbance less than 0.05 at the λex have been prepared. To determine the Φ, we used the following equation (1) [34]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for MTT assay
ce:section.ce:para:
Evaluation of the cytotoxicity of the multi-doped CDs was carried out. 1 x 105 cells/mL (WI-38 human lung fibroblast, VACSERA, Cairo, Egypt) were inoculated in a 96-well plate and cultured at 37°C under 5.0 % CO2 incubator for 24 h. A series of dilutions (1.56, 3.125, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, and 100 µg/mL) of the prepared multi-doped CDs were added to the plate in septuplets. Following a 24-hour incubation period, 20 µL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added to each well, followed by 4 h incubation. 100 µL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added into each well to dissolve the formed purple formazan crystals. The plate was read immediately by a plate reader (EXL 800, United states) at 570 nm. A blank experiment was done using well containing multi-doped CDs and MTT reagent without cells. Serial dilutions of doxorubicin (1.56, 3.125, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, and 100 µg/mL) have been similarly treated to compare the cytotoxicity. The percentage of relative cell viability was estimated using Eq. (2) [35]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for determination of TCC
Creation of calibration curve
ce:section.ce:para: Increasing quantities of the prepared solution of TCC (100 μg/mL) were added to a sequences of volumetric flasks measuring 10 mL followed by 1000 μL of BRB of pH 3.5 and 1000 μL of the multi-doped CDs working solution. The volumetric flasks were completed to the final volume with distilled water and well mixed. Then, the fluorescence was recorded at 416 nm (emission) after excitation at 350 nm. A control experiment (blank) was accomplished in the absence of drug and its fluorescence represented as (F0). Every experiment was carried out in triplicate. Eventually, the calibration curve was figured out by plotting Ln(F0/F) against TCC [μM], and the regression equation has been calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TCC in tablets
ce:section.ce:para: Ten tablets were accurately weighed, finely grinded, and well-mixed. An amount of the powder equivalent to 10 mg TCC was transferred to 100 mL volumetric flask, made up to volume with distilled water, sonicated for 30 min to ensure extraction of the active constituent, and filtered to obtain clear aqueous solution. Various quantities of the filtrate have been transferred to a group of 10 mL volumetric flasks then the procedure narrated under section 2.6.1 was followed to determine the concentrations and %recoveries of TCC in tablets. All experiments were done in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalysis experiments
ce:section.ce:para:
The degradation experiments were carried out in the presence of the prepared multi-doped CDs and the organic dye (EBT). 1000 μL of the multi-doped CDs was added to an aqueous solution of EBT (50 mL, 45x10−6 M). This solution was sonicated for 30 min to achieve adsorption/desorption equilibrium. The multi-doped CDs-EBT solution was exposed to different irradiation conditions (visible light, UV radiation at 365 nm, and darkness). A fixed volume of the reaction mixture was taken at 20 min regular time intervals for measuring the absorbance by UV–VIS spectrophotometer at 530 nm. Photodegradation efficiency (η%) was determined by Eq. (3) [36].
(3)

where A0 and At are the absorbance before and after the degradation of solution polluted by EBT, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Microwave-induced green synthesis of multi-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The approach for synthesis method is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The ultrafast microwave-induced synthesis of multi-doped CDs was established permitting completion of the synthesis process using modest energy from a typical household microwave at 900 W for 6 min.

Initially, a variety of ratios of expired VB1: sodium phosphate has been studied (1:10, 10:1, 1:1, and 2:1, w/w) with both microwave time (6 min) and starting solution's ultimate volume (20 mL) were kept constant. At the ratio of [1:1, w/w (0.1375 g:0.1375 g)], the maximum fluorescence intensity was obtained (Fig. S1a). Alternatively, the same ratios of VB1: dopant (1:10, 10:1, 1:1, and 2:1, w/w) have been studied using boric acid as auxiliary precursor instead of sodium orthophosphate. However, these mixtures produced CDs with much weaker fluorescence (Fig. S1a). Moreover, ternary mixtures containing VB1, sodium phosphate, and boric acid have been investigated by adding 0.025 g of boric acid to all previous mixtures containing VB1: sodium phosphate (1:1, 2:1, 10:1, and 1:10, w/w). The obtained results (Fig. S1b) revealed the negative influence of the addition of boric acid to the synthesis mixture. Thus, VB1: sodium phosphate (1:1, w/w) mixture is the best reactants combination for efficient synthesis of the multi-doped CDs.

For further optimization, the microwave irradiation time has also been studied using [1:1, w/w] ratio of VB1: sodium phosphate. The maximum fluorescence intensity was reached at 6 min (Fig. S1c) that was selected as the ideal microwave irradiation time for the synthesis of multi-doped CDs, which verify the time-efficiency of the established synthetic method.

Under these synthesis conditions, VB1 undergoes carbonization with the formation of CDs self-doped with nitrogen, sulfur, and chlorine. Furthermore, phosphate can covalently bind to the growing CDs surface and/or be adsorbed as a capping ligand. This resulted in efficient multiple doping of the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of the prepared CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological characterization and dimensions of the synthesized multi-doped CDs were examined using TEM imaging. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, the CDs exhibit superb dispersibility in water and spherical shape with average size of 12.32 ± 4.98 nm.

By using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), the composed elements of the multi-doped CDs were mapped. As illustrated in Fig. 2b, the EDX spectra revealed that carbon (42.97 %), oxygen (25.49 %), nitrogen (25.70 %), natrium (3.78 %), phosphorus (1.08 %), sulfur (0.23 %), and chlorine (0.75 %) elements were present which verified the composition and the multi-doping of the fabricated CDs. Furthermore, various paths of electron transition emerged because of the bandgap caused by phosphorus and chlorine co-doping, enhancing the fluorescence emission wavelength. This doping also shows perfect water dispersibility and stable photoluminescence. CDs' photoluminescence and catalytic efficacy can both be improved by chlorine doping since it is rich in electrons with high electronegativity. Thus, when doped into CDs, chlorine provides an extra level of electron energy leading to polychromatic fluorescence. Additionally, sulfur-chlorine doping can cause charge segregation in visible light since the reaction between the oxidizing/reducing agent creates reactive radical’s species. Additionally, electrons or holes are imprisoned at different sulfur-chlorine-CDs surface sites. Both radical species and electrons/holes exhibit photocatalytic degradation activity to organic dyes [37].

To pinpoint the surface functional groups of the fabricated multi-doped CDs, FT-IR spectroscopy was performed. As illustrated in Fig. 2c, there are multiple common absorption peaks. Strong peaks at 3833.16, 3738.31, and 3614.82 cm−1 are ascribed to N-H and O-H of free alcoholic and phenolic groups, and =CH aromatic stretching vibration, respectively. The peak at 2933.32 cm−1 associates with aliphatic –CH. The strong single peak at 2350 cm−1 is characteristic for C≡N stretching vibration. The two peaks at 1739.94 and 1694.58 cm−1 are attributed to C=C and C=N stretching vibration. The weak peak at 1526.11 cm−1 represents asymmetric stretching of N-O. Additionally, the peaks at the wavenumbers of 1108.30 and 1004.49 cm−1 are assigned to S=O. Very weak peaks at 1236.18, 872.74 and 823.40 cm−1 are referred to P=O and C-Cl stretching vibration, respectively [38].

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of the multi-doped CDs were captured to ascertain the binding states of the elements and the chemical composition of CDs surface [39]. As displayed in Fig. 2d the identity of the prominent peaks in the XPS survey scan were assigned to carbon (292.15 eV), oxygen (533.1 eV), nitrogen (407.01 eV), sulfur (160.93 eV), natrium (1073.11 eV), phosphorus (133.85 eV), and chlorine (204.27 eV), which implies the efficient multi-doping of the fabricated CDs. The obtained high resolution C1s spectrum scan (Fig. S2a) represents four peaks at 286.23, 289.07, 292.15, and 294.74 eV, that are pertinent to C-N/C-S/C-O/C-Cl/C-OH, O-C=O, π–π* and O=C-N, respectively [39 40 41]. The high-resolution spectra of N1s (Fig. S2b) can be deconvoluted into four peaks, two peaks at 399.86 and 403.1, which are attributed to cyano group (C≡N) and ammonium salt (NH4Cl), respectively. Another two peaks around 406–407 eV were assigned to nitrate ion (NO3 −) [39]. As illustrated in Fig. S2c, the high-resolution spectra of O1s show three characteristic peaks, the one with binding energy of 532.7 was assigned to PO3 2 -/SO4 2 -/OH and the two appeared at 535.2 and 538 eV refer to chemisorbed oxygen and/or H2O [39 42]. The high-resolution spectrum of S2p exhibits three distinctive peaks at 169.5, 165.52, and 168.8 that are assigned to SO4 2 -, thiophene and SO2 respectively (Fig. S2d) [37]. As seen in (Fig. S2e), the high-resolution spectrum of P2p displays peculiar peak at 133.86 eV which relates to P2p1/2. Moreover, the extra peaks observed at 140.73 and 139.44 may be the phosphorus and carbon association [43,44]. The two peaks observed around 203.7 and 205 eV in Cl2p high resolution spectrum were allude to Cl2p3/2 and Cl2p1/2 levels, respectively (Fig. S2f) [45]. The characteristic peak near 1073 eV in Na1s spectrum corresponds to Na-O association (Fig. S2g) [42]. Hence, the XPS analysis disclosed the multi-doping of nitrogen, oxygen, phosphorus, and sulfur heteroatoms into CDs.

Next, Zeta potential study was performed to scan the surface charge of the prepared CDs, yielding a value of −12.6 mV which confirms the presence of negatively charged functional groups. The repulsion between the negatively charged groups precludes their aggregation and stabilizing them.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the prepared CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the synthesized multi-doped CDs were elaborated using UV–VIS and fluorescence spectrophotometers. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, two well-resolved bands can be seen in the UV spectrum. The first one, near UV band, appears at 290 nm is attributed to n–π* transition of NH group that exists on the surface of the synthesized multi-doped CDs and the other, far UV band, observed at 223 nm is assigned to π-π* transition of the C = C of the conjugated aromatic center [46].

The multi-doped CDs exhibit strong blue fluorescence at 416 nm when excited at 350 nm (Fig. 3a). After being excited at wavelengths between 300 and 400 nm, the optimum excitation wavelength was observed at 350 nm (Fig. 3b). It was noted that increasing λex from 300 to 350 nm was associated with a corresponding increase in fluorescence intensity until reaching the maximum intensity at 350 nm. Further increase of λex from 360 to 400 nm resulted in a substantial decrease of the fluorescence intensity with a red shift of λem. As a result, the maximum FI was attained at λex/λem of 350/416 nm. The existence of multi-doped CDs with diversified sizes and surface energy traps may provide a comprehensible explanation for this excitation-dependent emission performance. Additionally, a larger CD can have a longer excitation wavelength due to its larger size. The number of excited particles affects the emission intensity at a given excitation wavelength. As the greatest number of particles were excited at 350 nm, the maximum fluorescence intensity from multi-doped CDs was obtained [47]. The quantum yield of the multi-doped CDs was determined to be 3.5 % using quinine sulfate as a reference. The fluorescence mechanisms in CDs mostly suggest that the fluorescence of CDs results from an interaction of diverse aspects that comprise a combination of the aromatic carbon core of the CDs, the surface defects, and the molecular state which can alter the band gap in CDs causing fluorescence emission [48]. The quantum yield of the prepared CDs is attributed to the high aromatic sp2 core and the amended nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, and oxygen functional groups (N-H, O-H, P=O, S=O, N-O) at the CDs surface [49]. These surface function groups comprise the surface defects that contribute to the high fluorescence of CDs due to the defect-related emissive traps initiating new energy levels and electron transition paths in the inter-band structure [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability study of the prepared CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Stability is an important consideration for CDs. Thus, we studied the influence of pH, utilizing BRB of pH 2–12, on the fluorescence of the synthesized CDs. As revealed by diagrammatic illustration in Fig. 3c, the pH does not have a dramatic effect on the fluorescence of CDs. The fluorescence of CDs was at highest value over pH 2–4, showing a slight decrease by about 12 % over pH 5–12. Hence, the reasonable stability of fluorescence over this wide range of pH is a prominent benefit.

As well, upon exposure of the multi-doped CDs to a variety of temperature degrees mimicking different climates (14–29 °C), no discernible change in the fluorescence intensity was detected (Fig. 3d) confirming their consistency within this relevant temperature range.

Additionally, a solution of CDs was continuously exposed to ultraviolet radiation at 365 nm to assess its sensitivity to photobleaching. The CDs exhibited good stability where only 11 % decline of fluorescence intensity was observed after 1 h of continuous exposure to UV light. Moreover, the multi-doped CDs solution exhibited excellent stability over 6 months at 4 °C without any obvious signs of turbidity or precipitation. These findings assert the excellent stability of synthesized multi-doped CDs and their tremendous potential to be used in a variety of real applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular toxicity of the multi-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of multi-doped CDs was evaluated by the MTT assay using WI-38 human lung fibroblast cells and the anticancer doxorubicin was utilized as a standard. As seen in Fig. S3 (supplementary material), different concentrations [1.56, 3.125, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, and 100 μg mL−1] of both multi-doped CDs and doxorubicin were seeds in a 96-well culture cell plate containing the WI-38 cell line and the effect was observed. It is concluded from the results represented in Fig. S3 (supplementary material) that the multi-doped CDs show very minor toxicity as compared to doxorubicin. For concentrations ≤ 6.25 μg mL−1 of the multi-doped CDs, 100 % cell viability was observed, while 50 % cell viability was detected in the case of doxorubicin. This evidences the suitability of the multi-doped CDs as a good candidate for various applications, such as pharmaceutical and environmental applications, with minimal toxicity and good biocompatibility [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparing the developed synthesis method with some reported techniques
ce:section.ce:para: From Table S2 (supplementary material), it is obvious that the proposed synthesis method is better than the methods that have been reported for CDs synthesis [51–56]. Since it needs less time for synthesis where it is 20–120 times faster than the reported hydrothermal methods. Besides, the great efficiency of the microwave synthesis technique is highlighted by the low cost, simplicity, wide availability, and ultrafast synthesis. Furthermore, the current method has significant advantages in that it uses the least amount of energy, and it completely avoids the use of organic solvents.

The utilization of a valueless expired pharmaceutical is another important advantage of the developed synthesis procedure. The “Valueless-to-Wealth” strategy offered the designed approach a noticeable greenness merit. Furthermore, this method is quick, one-step, affordable, cost-effective, and doesn’t impair the ecosystem so it is well-suited for broad applications.

The uniqueness of the synthesized CDs is prominent by virtue of their excellent photoluminescence properties, outstanding stability under various conditions of light, aqueous medium, pH, and temperature, as well as low toxicity, and excellent aqueous solubility. Ultimately, the wide variety of surface function groups and the multi-doping with several elements serve as a glaring merit that offers the fabricated multi-doped CDs efficacy and dual functionality as a sensor and photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesized CDs as a fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The excellent fluorescent character of the prepared multi-doped CDs promoted their use as sensitive fluorescence probe for pharmaceutical analysis of the non-fluorescent drug TCC. We found that the association between the multi-doped CDs and the drug resulted in quenching of the fluorescence signal of the CDs in a quantitative manner. Thus, an optimization study was conducted to develop a validated procedure for TCC determination, and a comprehensive study of the fluorescence quenching mechanism was done.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the experimental conditions for TCC determination
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental conditions were thoroughly investigated to achieve sensitive detection of TCC. Initially, BRB with pH ranges from 2.0 to 12.0 were used to examine the impact of pH. As shown in Fig. 3e, [F0/F] attained its highest value at pH 3.5, with a gradual slight decline at increasing pH. Consequently, the best pH was concluded to be 3.5. Next, the impact of the buffer volume has been investigated using 500–3000 µL (Fig. 3f). The best BRB volume at pH 3.5 has been determined to be 1000 µL. Finally, the amount of multi-doped CDs (200–1300 µL) has been examined. Hence, 1000 µL was chosen as the optimum volume because it provided the best quenching efficiency (Fig. 3g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of quenching of the multi-doped CDs fluorescence by TCC
ce:section.ce:para: As seen in Fig. S4 a (supplementary material), the multi-doped CDs exhibit a high fluorescence intensity that was noticeably reduced in the presence of TCC (quencher). Increasing concentrations of TCC gradually attenuated the fluorescence intensity of the multi-doped CDs. Such quenching behavior may be attributed to a variety of mechanisms such as the inner filter effect (IFE), dynamic, or static quenching [57].

First, the possibility of including the IFE has been studied. Both the excitation, emission and UV–Visible spectra have been scanned. As clearly shown in Fig. 3a, the absorption spectrum of TCC (quencher) displays a strong overlap with the emission and minor part of quencher overlaps with the excitation of CDs revealing the potentiality for primary and secondary IFE due to the ability of TCC to absorb the excitation and emission wavelengths, respectively [22].

To reinforce the proposed mechanism, each value of the Fl intensity of the multi-doped CDs in the existence of increasing concentrations of the TCC was corrected for potential IFE in accordance with Eq. (4) [22].
(4)
F
c o r r e c t e d

F
o b s e r v e d
× a n t i l o g
A
e x
+
A
e m
2

Where Fcorrected is the fluorescence intensity that has been corrected after IFE has been removed, Fobserved is the measured fluorescence intensity, and Aex and Aem are the absorbance of the TCC at the Ex and Em wavelengths of multi-doped CDs, respectively.

Following this, the corrected and observed fluorescence suppressed efficiency (%E) were calculated using Eq. (5) [22] and plotted versus TCC concentration (Fig. S4b, supplementary material).
(5)
% E =
1 - (
F
F 0
)
×

The findings shown in Fig. S4b, demonstrate that IFE is the primary cause of the fluorescence quenching by approximately 70 % of the total suppressed efficiency. However, other mechanism(s) may oversee the remaining segment of quenching. Further investigation of fluorescence quenching by Stern-Volmer analysis provides additional proof of the quenching mechanisms. As illustrated in Fig. S4c (supplementary material), Stern-Volmer’s study has been carried out at different temperatures, 304, 323 and 342 K, and F0/F values were plotted versus TCC concentration according to Eq. (6) [23].
(6)
F
0
F
= 1 + k
D
T C C

Where
k D
stands for dynamic quenching constant of Stern-Volmer and [TCC] is the molar concentration of TCC. The acquired results showed that the Stern-Volmer graph is linear throughout the studied temperatures within the concentration range of 0–35.5 µM. Furthermore, we found that the slope of the Stern-Volmer graph (
k D
) is temperature-sensitive, with the KD value increasing consistently as the temperature rises (Table S3, supplementary material). This is a hallmark of dynamic quenching because higher temperatures increase collisional quenching. Dynamic quenching occurs when the excited state of fluorophore (CDs) collides with a quencher and returns to the ground state [23]. The higher the temperature, the easier for molecules to diffuse and collide which leads to an increase in KD [23]. Thus, combining all the evidence stated above, it is concluded that the fluorescence quenching of multi-doped CDs by TCC is due to a combination of IFE and dynamic quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the multi-doped CDs-based analytical method for TCC analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
Adhering to the International Council for Harmonization (ICH) Q2(R2) guidelines for the validation of analytical procedures, the proposed method was validated [58]. The plot of Ln (F0 /F) versus the concentration of TCC showed excellent linear relationship over the concentration range of 3.5 to 35.5 μM, correlation co-efficient r2 = 0.9998 (Fig. S4a, supplementary material), and with the regression equation:
(7) Ln (F0 / F) = 0.03C + 0.001

Where the symbol (C) represents the concentration of TCC (μM). Both the limit of quantification (LOQ) and limit of detection (LOD) were calculated by applying 10σ/s and 3.3σ/s, respectively. where (σ) stands for the blank standard deviation and (s) is the calibration curve slope. The calculated values of LOQ equal to 1.51 and LOD equal to 0.50 μM. A summary of the linearity data is presented in Table 1 .

Moreover, the accuracy and precision of the proposed method have been assessed based on inter-day and intra-day levels. To assess the method's intra-day accuracy and precision, three concentrations of TCC have been repeatedly measured in the same day for three successive times while for inter-day three concentrations of TCC have been repeatedly measured for three consecutive days. The calculated values of standard deviation (SD) and relative standard deviation (%RSD) demonstrate the high precision of our assay methodology, and the high % recovery (98.9–100.98 %) indicates the accuracy of the method as displayed in Table 1.

Furthermore, the outcomes of determination of TCC obtained using the designed probe have been compared with the results of a reported spectrophotometric method [20] using Variance ratio F–test and Student t-test [59]. As indicated by Table 1, there is no statistically significant difference between the two approaches since the computed t- and F-values are smaller than the theoretical values [59]. These findings show that the synthesized nanoprobe meets all validation requirements established by the ICH guidelines [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the multi-doped CDs nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is a significant issue to consider when evaluating the efficacy of the synthesized fluorescence probe. To assess the specificity of the nanoprobe, the potential interference from various possibly co-existing materials was studied. Table S4 (supplementary material) shows that in the presence of various cations such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Fe3 + and Cd2+, anions such as CO3 2−, HCO3 −, SO4 2−, and Cl−, organic substances such as sucrose, glucose, starch, methyl cellulose, sodium citrate, citric acid, and some drugs likely to be co-formulated or co-administered with TCC including paracetamol, aceclofenac, and spiramycin, there was no discernible change in the FI of the multi-doped CDs-TCC system. The percentage change in the fluorescence was between + 4.16 and −5.62 % demonstrating the high selectivity of the synthesized nanoprobe for TCC, allowing its use in pharmaceutical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the designed probe for analysis of TCC tablets
ce:section.ce:para: The designed fluorescence nanosensor has been assessed to confirm its suitability for real applications. It was applied for determination of TCC in tablets dosage form with excellent % recovery of 99.95 ± 1.55 % indicating high efficiency and selectivity of the developed method for TCC. The results of the developed method have been compared with that of a reported spectrophotometric method [20] by student t-test and variance ratio F-test [59], Table 1 demonstrates no significant difference respecting accuracy and precision, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the multi-doped CDs with the reported analytical method for the tested analytes
ce:section.ce:para: There are several merits that have been revealed when comparing the multi-doped CDs probe's performance for determining TCC to the reported analytical methods (Table S5). For the determination of TCC, the suggested approach is much more sensitive than the published methods [13–20]. Another excellent feature of the suggested probe is its eco-friendliness, safety, and rapidness. All the reported methods for TCC make extensive use of harmful and expensive chemicals and organic solvents, including acetic acid, methanol, ethyl acetate and acetonitrile. In addition to the longer time needed for equilibration, conditioning, assessment, and cleaning in HPLC technique. In contrast, the suggested method does not require these cost-prohibitive and potentially hazardous substances. The suggested method depends on a mix-and-read approach, no extra steps are needed for derivatization, heating, or extraction. This added significant advantages to the developed methodology compared to reported literature. Besides, the reported method is the first fluorimetry method to determine TCC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesized CDs as a photocatalyst for water remediation
ce:section.ce:para: Since the issue of water deterioration brought on by large-scale industrial wastewater discharge has drawn a lot of attention, numerous studies have focused on this topic because water contamination and its effects are currently a global problem [60]. Now, organic dyes make up a sizeable portion of industrial effluent due to their widespread use and large-scale manufacture. Furthermore, dye wastewater has emerged as one of the challenges to treat due to its massive discharge and poor biodegradability [25,26]. Hence, using photocatalytic degradation with nanomaterial as a catalyst is a successful and efficient approach [60]. In the present study, the synthesized multi-doped CDs possess a brilliant visible light-induced photocatalytic degradation towards EBT as an economical and eco-friendly photocatalytic agent without any jointure to any semiconductor nanomaterial. This offers the privilege of using them for remediation of wastewater.

The inclusion of surface functional groups on the multi-doped CDs facilitated their photocatalytic activity. Briefly, when electrons in the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) known as valance band (VB) are photo-excited to move to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LOMO) known as conduction band (CB) a photon is absorbed, which causes the production of oxidative holes (
h
+
) and the reduction of electrons (e−) in both HOMO and LOMO, respectively. Following this, it has been shown that the electron vacancies or holes remaining in the VB lead to the formation of e-h pairs. These e-h couples are relocated to the surface of the photocatalyst as O2
. and OH . which are generated over the reaction of e− and h + by O2 and H2O molecules, respectively [61,62].

Herein, a full investigation of the photocatalytic activity of the prepared multi-doped CDs has been conducted using photodegradation of EBT in aqueous solution. The degradation of EBT was estimated by monitoring the change in its UV–VIS absorption with reaction time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of photocatalytic activity of the prepared multi-doped CDs under different illumination conditions
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the photocatalytic capability of the prepared multi-doped CDs was studied in the presence of EBT dye using initial concentration of 45 μM as the target model pollutant. The degradation of EBT was carried out under three different conditions including artificial visible light (Fig. S5 a), UV light (Fig. S5b), and dark (Fig. S5c) up to 100 min. It was observed that after 100 min, the multi-doped CDs in the dark led to 46.5 % degradation of the EBT, while the dye degradation efficiency increased to 71.6 % upon exposure to UV-light at 365 nm. Interestingly, under visible light irradiation, the photodegradation efficiency of EBT reached 88 % (Fig. 4a). Therefore, we can symbolize these results in order from the best to the least as visible light > UV light > dark.
Next, the impact of different factors e.g., EBT concentration, CDs amount, and pH on the rate of degradation of EBT under visible light condition has been investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of EBT concentration
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of the initial dye concentration on the rate of degradation was evaluated upon exposure to visible light in the presence of a constant concentration of the synthesized multi-doped CDs. Different initial concentrations (45, 67.5, and 135 μM) were tried to assess the photocatalytic activity while the CDs amount remained constant. The test solutions were exposed to visible light and samples were withdrawn at 20 min time intervals for measuring their absorbance. According to the results obtained from Fig. S6a, maximum degradation was at the initial EBT concentration of 45 μM. A decline of the degradation efficiency of EBT toke place as the initial concentration of EBT increased. This is attributed to adsorption of the excess dye molecules on the CDs surface reducing the available active sites for photocatalysis. Besides, the increased dye concentration requires more radicals to be degraded; however, the amount of the formed radicals is constant as the CDs dose is constant. Thus, the amount of the generated radicals is not sufficient for the degradation of the dye at high concentration. Therefore, the photodegradation effectiveness decreased as the concentration of the dye increased [62]. This was confirmed by utilization of three-fold of CDs (3000 µL) with higher dye concertation (135 μM) that led to efficient degradation of about 49.6 % of the initial EBT concentration (Fig. S6b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on degradation efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: The pH has a prominent effect on photocatalytic degradation since it controls the surface potential of the photocatalyst, the degradation reaction and radicals’ generation [61]. Initially, the influence of different pHs (4.0, 7.0, and 10.0) on the photocatalytic degradation of EBT [45 μM] was studied in the presence of a fixed concentration of the catalyst under visible light radiation. The absorbance of the dye solution was measured at regular time intervals. By following Eq. (3) to determine the % degradation of the dye at each pH, the calculated results are shown in Fig. S6c. The graph shows that the % degradation is maximum (88 %) in neutral medium (pH = 7.0) compared to lower % degradation in acidic (pH = 4.0) and alkaline (pH = 10.0) media with observed values of 35.7 % and 3 %, respectively.

The effect of pH on the photocatalytic degradation of the dye is explained by its impact on how radical species, especially hydroxyl radicals are produced. These free radicals are created at the surface of the catalyst because of the oxidation of OH− or H2O by the generated holes (h+) according to Eqs. (8) and (9).
(8) OH−+h+ → ⋅OH
(9) H2O + h+ → ⋅OH + H+

O H
.
known as a strong oxidizing agent which can easily target the azo group [-N=N-] in EBT molecule and degrade it [63]. As EBT degrades, the functional groups become more negative, serving as a helpful element in electronic transitions [64].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the catalyst dose
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the photocatalyst dose was studied while fixing other experimental conditions (EBT 45 μM, pH 7.0). The resultant solutions were subjected to visible light and their absorbance was monitored at regular time intervals up to 80 min. Using Eq. (3), it was possible to calculate the % degradation of the dye in each solution. From Fig. S6d, as the dose of CDs increases, the % degradation of EBT increased. This is attributed to the increased surface area of the photocatalyst at high doses which in turn increases the generated free radicals and the % degradation of the dye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetics study of the degradation of EBT by CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the effect of visible light, the multi-doped CDs-induced photocatalytic degradation of EBT was investigated to reveal the kinetics of this reaction. By plotting ln (C0/Ct) versus time t, a linear fit was obtained for all plotted curves (Fig. 4b-e). These findings indicate that the photocatalytic degradation of the dye with time is based on pseudo-first-order kinetic in accordance with Eq. (10) [62].
(10)
l n
C 0
C t
= K t
Where C0 represents the initial concentration and Ct stands for the concentration at a certain time. Meanwhile K is the degradation rate constant with unit of min−1, determined from the slope of the
( l n
C 0
C t
)
versus time plot. The results of the kinetic study conducted under various conditions are outlined in Table 2 , where the reaction rate constant for the dye degradation under the optimized conditions was 0.0238 min−1 and the t1/2 equals 29.12 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of photocatalytic degradation
ce:section.ce:para:
There are several parameters that influence photocatalytic activity, such as band position, electron transfer, absorption irradiation, and photogenerated carrier efficiency [62]. The photocatalytic activity of the CDs is supported mainly by [65]:
(i)
The possibility of π-π* interaction with the dye noting that, the adsorbed dye molecules on the surface of the photocatalyst are the only molecules to be affected by the photocatalytic effect of CDs and not the bulk solution of the dye.
(ii)
The relatively low band gap energy and λexcitation facilitate the formation of h+/e− pair that helps the formation of O2
•
− and HO • radicals. The dye interaction with the surface function groups of the CDs under light irradiation generate h+/e− pairs. The produced holes convert H2O and HO− to HO • or directly oxidize the dye molecules. Reactive oxygen species O2
•
− and the h+ oxidize EBT resulting in the formation of water and carbon dioxide. These effects are explained in Eqs. (11)–(17) and Fig. 5a, showed a schematic diagram of the photocatalysis process.
(11) CD + hv → CD* (h+ + e−)
(12) O2 + e− → O2
•
−
(13) 2H+ + e− + O2 → H2O2
(14) H2O2 + O2
•
− → HO •
(15) EBT + HO • → Degradation
(16) EBT + O2 · → H2O + CO2
(17) EBT + h+ → H2O + CO2

The photocatalytic degradation of EBT with the multi-doped CDs has been investigated by applying a radical scavenging technique that entailed adding different appropriate trapping agents to delve into the mechanism and reactive species responsible for the degradation. Subsequently, isopropanol (IPA, 10 mM), benzophenone (BP, 10 mM), and NaHCO3 (10 mM) were employed as HO • , O2
.
−, and h+ scavengers, respectively [66]. As illustrated in Fig. 5b, the efficiency of dye degradation has significantly dropped from 88 % to 61.08 %, 24.12 % and 3.45 %, respectively, upon addition of IPA, BP, and NaHCO3, respectively. This indicates that HO • , O2
.
−, and h+ play an important role in the degradation process. The influential order of the radicals is: h+ > O2
.
− > HO • . Meanwhile, the h+ is considered the principal reactant throughout the dye decomposition.

It is considered that the electron rich nature of chlorine, sulfur, and phosphorus dopants helps in altering the prepared CDs towards more negative potential and enhances its reductive capability. This reductive character of the photocatalyst sources electrons even in dark conditions, which are moved across the surface causing the formation of reactive oxygen species like HO• and O2 • those cause degradation of even the dye in the dark [67]. The degradation of EBT under light and dark is remarkably vital in the perspective of wastewater treatment focusing on the colored run-offs of the dye industry, for example.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the prepared multi-doped CDs with reported photocatalyst for dye decontamination
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the efficacy, distinctiveness, and additional benefits of the synthesized CDs as a photocatalyst, we conducted a comparative investigation of the CDs synthesized by our new approach and the CDs that have been previously reported for the degradation of organic dyes in water [25,68–74].The presented multi-doped CDs are synthesized from carbon precursors not from a metal source which represents a major advantage owing to the disadvantages of metal nanoparticles that include their instability, potential toxicity to human, difficulties in their synthesis, and contamination that may occur during the synthesis process [75]. Moreover, the prepared CDs efficiently decomposed the tested organic dye within the shortest time with the best degradation efficiency (Table S6). This clarifies the high efficiency of the prepared CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the greenness of the developed approach
ce:section.ce:para: Green chemistry attempts to find and produce inexpensive chemical products and procedures that decrease pollution at its source, attaining the greatest level of the pollution-preclusion hierarchy [76]. The adoption of an efficient CDs synthesis technique that requires less energy and short synthesis time using expired chemicals that are no longer effective and their transformation into worthwhile value plays a significant role in protecting the ecosystem from pollution. This significantly contributes to the greenness, safety, as well as cost-effectiveness of the devised analytical approach. In addition, utilization of the valueless-to-wealth strategy, high sample throughput, absence of any organic solvent, mix-read approach, energy-efficiency, time-efficiency, and using a household microwave, corroborate the method greenness. For evaluating the greenness of the developed approach, specialized tools are needed for the assessment of greenness criteria. Therefore, we were utilized an analytical GREEnness calculator (AGREE) [77] a thorough, adaptable, and uncomplicated evaluation method that yields a readily comprehensible and instructive outcome by investigating the reagent amounts and toxicity, waste generated, energy requirements, and number of feasible stages. As stated by Fig. S7a, the score of 0.69 obtained from AGREE software indicates the excellent greenness of the methodology. In addition to the complementary green analytical procedure index (ComplexGAPI) [78] was also applied to assess the greenness of the developed approach. It is primarily focused on evaluating the method of analysis involving the utilization of materials developed prior to the analytical step, therefore it has been applied to evaluate the proposed analytical method. The graphic representation displayed in Fig. S7b depicts the ComplexGAPI of the developed method that reveals its green feature. The bottom hexagonal pictogram symbolizes the “green” nature of the pre-analysis step that refers to the synthesis of the CDs. It entails assessing the yield, reagents, solvents, energy, occupational hazard, technical setup, workup, purity, and waste.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: A new efficient strategy has been validated to reuse expired pharmaceuticals rather than discarding them and deteriorating the ecosystem. Herein, we generate a unique light-responsive and fluorescent nanosensor multi-doped CDs from expired VB1 without consumption of any organic solvents via modest, efficient, economic, and ultrafast one-pot microwave synthesis method. The structure, self-doping, and properties of the multi-doped CDs were demonstrated through EDX analysis, XPS study, FTIR spectroscopy, Zeta potential, UV–VIS, fluorescence spectroscopy, and TEM imaging. The multi-doped CDs exhibit significant efficacy and dual functionality as a fluorescent nanoprobe as well as a photocatalyst. It was applied as a fluorescent nanoprobe for sensitive determination of the muscle relaxant drug (TCC) over the concentration range of 3.5–35.5 μM with limit of detection 0.50 μM via synergetic IFE and dynamic quenching mechanisms. Furthermore, the prepared multi-doped CDs were applied as effective photocatalyst for degradation of EBT dye, as an example of pollutant under visible light condition. The degradation of EBT was achieved up to 88 % within 80 min following pseudo-first order kinetic. The main reactive species can be ordered according to their contribution in the degradation process as: holes > oxygen radicals > hydroxyl radicals, and a probable mechanistic pathway was anticipated based on radical-scavenging experiments. This study offers fresh perspectives on developing new visible light sensitive photocatalysts for the removal of dye effluent in the environment.
